subtopic_detail: Lactational mastitis versus inflammatory breast cancer
stem: A 34-year-old breastfeeding mother presents to the GP 6 weeks postpartum with a 5 day history of right breast pain, redness, and warmth that has progressed despite frequent feeding and warm compresses. The right breast skin shows diffuse erythema involving more than one quadrant, with non-tender peau d orange in the upper outer area but no discrete fluctuant lump. She is afebrile and clinically well. She has tried paracetamol and continued breastfeeding without resolution. There is no nipple discharge.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse erythema upper outer quadrant right breast with peau d orange; non-tender; no discrete mass; no axillary lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ultrasound shows no abscess; skin and milk swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently to a breast clinic for assessment including ultrasound, mammogram, and core biopsy
options.rationale: Skin changes (peau d orange, persistent erythema) that fail to respond to antibiotic therapy require breast clinic referral to exclude inflammatory breast cancer. (Source: RACGP: Breast cancer red flags)

options.text: Start oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days and review in 7 days as needed
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy of persistent peau d orange without trial of response and no clear infection delays evaluation for inflammatory carcinoma. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: lactational mastitis)

options.text: Encourage cessation of breastfeeding to allow resolution and review the patient in 1 week time
options.rationale: Cessation of breastfeeding is not first-line and does not address inflammatory cancer if present. (Source: RANZCOG: Breastfeeding and lactation)

options.text: Empirical incision and drainage in the emergency department for suspected occult breast abscess
options.rationale: Bedside ultrasound is negative for abscess; surgical drainage without collection risks needless intervention. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: lactational mastitis)

options.text: Hot compresses, milk expression, and follow up in 4 weeks without further investigation now
options.rationale: Persistent peau d orange beyond a normal mastitis course is a red flag and warrants urgent breast clinic referral. (Source: RACGP: Breast cancer red flags)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis is common in the first 3 months postpartum and typically presents with focal breast erythema, swelling, and systemic symptoms responsive to milk drainage and 24 to 48 hours of oral flucloxacillin or cefalexin if not improving. Failure to respond to antibiotics, persistent or diffuse erythema, peau d orange, skin tethering, or a discrete mass should prompt urgent breast clinic referral to exclude inflammatory breast cancer or other malignancy. Inflammatory breast cancer is rare but rapidly progressive, often mimics infection, and requires triple assessment (clinical examination, imaging, and core biopsy). Continuing breastfeeding during mastitis is encouraged because it aids resolution.
key_points: Lactational mastitis is managed with milk drainage and flucloxacillin or cefalexin if not improving
Persistent peau d orange or non-response to antibiotics prompts triple assessment to exclude inflammatory breast cancer
Continuing breastfeeding usually helps resolve mastitis
explanation.pearls: Inflammatory breast cancer presents with diffuse erythema, peau d orange and no discrete mass; it accounts for under 5% of breast cancers but has the worst prognosis if missed.

subtopic_detail: OASIS classification and immediate repair planning
stem: A 28-year-old woman has just delivered a 3.8 kg infant via spontaneous vaginal delivery with vacuum assistance at 40 weeks gestation. On perineal examination by the obstetric registrar, there is a tear through the perineal skin, perineal muscles, external anal sphincter, internal anal sphincter, and rectal mucosa. The vacuum was used because of fetal distress. She has an epidural in situ and is haemodynamically stable. The consultant has been called to attend.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/80, HR 86, T 36.9, blood loss 350 mL
data_table.Examination: Tear extending through external and internal anal sphincters and rectal mucosa, epidural functional
data_table.Investigations: Not currently indicated; recheck Hb in 6 hours postpartum

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repair only the perineal skin and external anal sphincter in delivery suite with local anaesthesia
options.rationale: Incomplete repair of internal sphincter and rectal mucosa leads to fistula and incontinence; full theatre repair is required. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS guideline)

options.text: Repair the tear at the bedside in delivery suite using local anaesthetic infiltration only
options.rationale: Bedside repair under local infiltration does not provide adequate visualisation or sphincter approximation for OASIS. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS guideline)

options.text: Leave the tear unrepaired and review by colorectal surgery in clinic at 2 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Unrepaired sphincter injury leads to incontinence and infection; theatre repair is the standard of care. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS guideline)

options.text: Refer urgently for primary defunctioning colostomy by colorectal surgery this admission
options.rationale: Defunctioning colostomy is reserved for complex or breakdown injuries, not primary OASIS repair. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS guideline)

options.text: Transfer to theatre for repair under regional anaesthesia with antibiotic prophylaxis cover
options.rationale: OASIS injury (3c or 4th degree tears) is repaired in theatre by a trained operator with antibiotic prophylaxis and laxatives to optimise functional outcome. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS guideline)

explanation.summary: Obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASIS) include third degree tears (3a less than 50% external sphincter, 3b more than 50% external sphincter, 3c involving internal sphincter) and fourth degree tears (involving rectal mucosa). Repair is performed in theatre by a trained operator under regional or general anaesthesia with broad spectrum antibiotic prophylaxis, end-to-end or overlap technique for external sphincter, mattress sutures for internal sphincter, and continuous closure of rectal mucosa with absorbable sutures. Postoperative management includes laxatives, pelvic floor physiotherapy, and follow up with perineal clinic at 6 to 12 weeks. Future deliveries are discussed in a perineal clinic with reference to symptoms and anal manometry.
key_points: OASIS includes 3a, 3b, 3c (internal sphincter), and 4th degree (rectal mucosa) tears
Repair occurs in theatre with antibiotic prophylaxis and postoperative laxatives
Future delivery planning depends on symptoms, anal manometry, and ultrasound
explanation.pearls: Antibiotic prophylaxis for OASIS uses intravenous cefazolin and metronidazole at induction, with at least 5 days of oral cefalexin and metronidazole after repair to reduce wound infection and breakdown.

sources.label: RCOG GTG 29: Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

subtopic_detail: Maternal sepsis: hour-1 bundle in pregnancy
stem: A 27-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with rigors, lower abdominal pain, and foul smelling vaginal loss for 12 hours. Her temperature is 39.1 degrees Celsius, heart rate 130, respiratory rate 26, blood pressure 92/56, oxygen saturation 95% on room air. The cardiotocograph shows a baseline of 175 with absent variability and frequent uterine contractions. She is 4 cm dilated on examination, with cloudy fluid noted. Bedside lactate is 4.2 mmol/L.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, cloudy malodorous liquor draining, uterine tenderness, fetal tachycardia
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L, WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 250 mg/L, blood cultures taken, urine taken, MSU pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous tocolytic therapy with terbutaline to prolong pregnancy and reduce uterine activity
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in maternal sepsis and may worsen haemodynamic instability. (Source: SOMANZ: Maternal sepsis)

options.text: Immediate emergency caesarean section without antibiotic dose first being administered
options.rationale: Delivery considerations follow resuscitation and antibiotic administration; antibiotics within 1 hour reduce maternal and fetal mortality. (Source: SOMANZ: Maternal sepsis)

options.text: Intravenous fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg and intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam within 1 hour
options.rationale: Maternal sepsis is managed with sepsis hour-1 bundle including blood cultures, broad spectrum antibiotics, IV fluids 30 mL/kg, and source control planning. (Source: SOMANZ: Maternal sepsis)

options.text: Intravenous antibiotics after blood culture growth and identification of causative organism
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics until cultures grow increases mortality in sepsis; empirical broad spectrum cover is given within 1 hour. (Source: SOMANZ: Maternal sepsis)

options.text: Single dose intravenous corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity before any other intervention
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are part of the obstetric management plan once mother is stabilising, not the first priority over resuscitation. (Source: SOMANZ: Maternal sepsis)

explanation.summary: Maternal sepsis carries high maternal and fetal mortality and is recognised early using maternal early warning systems. SOMANZ guidance applies the hour-1 bundle in obstetric settings: serum lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics where possible, broad spectrum empirical antibiotics within 1 hour (piperacillin-tazobactam with consideration of clindamycin for streptococcal toxic shock), 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation, source control assessment, and noradrenaline for fluid-refractory hypotension. Source control for chorioamnionitis is delivery; for septic abortion is uterine evacuation. Multidisciplinary involvement of obstetrics, intensive care, neonatology, and microbiology is essential.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle (cultures, antibiotics, fluids, source control assessment) applies in pregnancy
Piperacillin-tazobactam with clindamycin if streptococcal toxic shock suspected
Source control for chorioamnionitis is delivery rather than continued conservative management
explanation.pearls: Add intravenous clindamycin to broad spectrum cover in suspected streptococcal toxic shock syndrome to reduce toxin production; continue until clinical response is established.

sources.label: RCOG GTG 64: Bacterial sepsis in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Anovulatory infertility in PCOS: ovulation induction agent
stem: A 28-year-old woman with a BMI of 31 has been trying to conceive for 14 months with her partner. Her cycles are irregular at 35 to 60 days. She has clinical hyperandrogenism with acne and hirsutism. Pelvic ultrasound shows polycystic ovarian morphology. Her partner has a normal semen analysis. Her thyroid function and prolactin are normal. Her HbA1c is 5.4%, fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L, and she has had a normal hysterosalpingogram. She declines fertility supplements and tracks ovulation reliably.

data_table.Examination: Mild acne and hirsutism, no acanthosis nigricans, otherwise normal pelvic exam
data_table.Investigations: TSH normal, prolactin normal, day 21 progesterone consistent with anovulation; partner semen analysis normal; HSG patent tubes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line ovulation induction agent?

options.text: Metformin 1500 mg daily monotherapy without additional ovulation induction agent in cycle
options.rationale: Metformin alone produces lower ovulation and pregnancy rates than letrozole in PCOS-related infertility. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Oral clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 as standard first-line therapy
options.rationale: Clomiphene remains effective but is now positioned behind letrozole in current AU and international guidance. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Oral letrozole 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 with monitoring of follicular response
options.rationale: AU and international PCOS guidance prefers letrozole over clomiphene for first-line ovulation induction in anovulatory PCOS infertility because of higher live birth rates. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Gonadotropin injections starting low dose without trial of oral ovulation induction first
options.rationale: Gonadotropins are second-line after failure of oral agents because of higher multiple pregnancy and OHSS risk. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Direct referral for in vitro fertilisation without trial of ovulation induction agents first
options.rationale: IVF is reserved for failure of ovulation induction, tubal disease, or other infertility indications. (Source: International evidence-based guideline for PCOS)

explanation.summary: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is diagnosed by the Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3: oligo or anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology) after exclusion of other causes. The international evidence-based PCOS guideline (with significant AU input) recommends letrozole as the first-line ovulation induction agent in anovulatory PCOS infertility because of higher live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy rates compared to clomiphene. Clomiphene or metformin remain alternatives, and combined letrozole-metformin is considered in higher BMI. Gonadotropins or IVF are second and third line. Pre-conception screening for diabetes and lifestyle measures including weight loss of 5 to 10% improve ovulation and pregnancy outcomes.
key_points: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS-related infertility per current guidelines
Pre-conception screening (OGTT, BP, thyroid) is part of PCOS workup
Pre-conception weight loss of 5 to 10% improves ovulation and live birth
explanation.pearls: Letrozole 2.5 to 7.5 mg daily for 5 days starting day 2 to 5 of cycle achieves ovulation in around 75% of PCOS women with higher live birth rates than clomiphene.

subtopic_detail: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax: ambulatory device versus chest drain
stem: A 22-year-old tall thin male non-smoker presents with sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea over the past 6 hours. He has no past medical history and is on no medications. He is haemodynamically stable. Chest radiograph shows a 3.5 cm rim of air at the level of the hilum with no shift of the trachea or mediastinum. He has no history of underlying lung disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 92, RR 20, SpO2 97% room air, T 36.7
data_table.Examination: Reduced air entry and hyperresonance over the right hemithorax, trachea midline, no surgical emphysema
data_table.Investigations: CXR right-sided pneumothorax with 3.5 cm rim of air at hilum; no rib fractures; no underlying lung disease

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Observation only on supplemental high flow oxygen for 24 hours without aspiration or drain
options.rationale: Observation alone for a large primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a symptomatic patient is no longer routine first-line; ambulatory drain or aspiration is preferred. (Source: BTS pleural disease guideline 2023)

options.text: Insertion of an ambulatory pneumothorax drain with Heimlich valve and discharge planning
options.rationale: Current British Thoracic Society 2023 guidance supports ambulatory management of large primary spontaneous pneumothorax in selected stable patients. (Source: BTS pleural disease guideline 2023)

options.text: Needle thoracocentesis at the second intercostal space midclavicular line without imaging review
options.rationale: Needle thoracocentesis is reserved for tension pneumothorax with haemodynamic compromise. (Source: BTS pleural disease guideline 2023)

options.text: Insertion of a 28 French chest drain and admission to the high dependency unit for 5 days
options.rationale: Large bore chest drains are reserved for secondary pneumothorax or trauma; primary spontaneous pneumothorax is managed with smaller drains or ambulatory devices. (Source: BTS pleural disease guideline 2023)

options.text: Referral for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgical pleurodesis on the first presentation now
options.rationale: Surgical pleurodesis is for recurrent, bilateral, persistent air leak, or high-risk occupation pneumothorax, not first presentation. (Source: BTS pleural disease guideline 2023)

explanation.summary: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax management has shifted to a symptom-led approach: stable patients with minimal symptoms may be observed, while symptomatic patients with large pneumothoraces are increasingly managed with ambulatory pneumothorax devices or needle aspiration rather than admission with a chest drain. Tension pneumothorax (haemodynamic compromise, tracheal deviation) is decompressed immediately by needle thoracostomy followed by chest drain. Secondary pneumothorax (underlying lung disease) usually warrants admission and chest drain. Recurrence prevention with VATS and pleurodesis is considered for recurrent disease, persistent air leak over 5 days, bilateral disease, or high-risk occupations (pilots, divers).
key_points: Symptom-led approach replaces strict size cut-offs for primary spontaneous pneumothorax
Ambulatory pneumothorax devices are an option for stable symptomatic primary disease
Surgical pleurodesis is reserved for recurrence, persistent air leak, bilateral disease, or high-risk occupation
explanation.pearls: Needle thoracostomy is reserved for tension pneumothorax with haemodynamic compromise; in clinical practice it is replaced by finger thoracostomy in many AU prehospital settings.
